skill.name: summon-daem0n
skill.description: Guide for configuring Daem0n-MCP memory, linked repositories, search behavior, and Claude hook automation.
skill.summary: Set up Daem0n-MCP memory workflows across single or multi-repository projects.
skill.author: DasBluEyedDevil

security_audit.summary: Most static Ruby backtick findings are false positives caused by Markdown fences and inline examples, not executable Ruby code. The skill still presents high risk because it instructs users to install Claude hooks that automatically run local Python commands during edit, write, and stop events. It also includes a destructive hidden-directory removal command, but no evidence found for weak cryptography, network reconnaissance, system reconnaissance, credential exfiltration, or prompt injection.

high_findings.title: Automatic Command Hooks for AI Tool Events
high_findings.description: The skill instructs users to place command hooks in .claude/settings.json that run Python scripts from $HOME/Daem0nMCP before edits, after writes, and when a session stops. This can cause unreviewed local scripts to execute automatically during normal AI tool use. The behavior may be legitimate for Daem0n-MCP, but it needs manual review before marketplace publication.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: The hook configuration explicitly uses command entries that execute Python scripts on PreToolUse, PostToolUse, and Stop events. The intent is automation, but the referenced scripts are outside this skill file and cannot be verified here.

medium_findings.title: Destructive Hidden Data Directory Removal
medium_findings.description: The skill includes a reset step that runs recursive deletion against /repos/.daem0nmcp. The static root or home deletion label is a false positive because the target is a project data directory, but the command can still permanently remove hidden Daem0n-MCP state.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The exact command is present and destructive. The risk is limited by the specific example path, but users may copy or adapt it incorrectly.

medium_findings.title: Hidden Configuration and State File Access
medium_findings.description: The skill directs users to copy hidden .daem0nmcp data and to edit .claude/settings.json. These actions match the skill purpose, but they affect hidden project state and AI tool configuration that can change future automation behavior.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The hidden paths are explicitly named and the operational impact is clear. The actions are expected for setup, so this is a controlled configuration risk rather than evidence of malware.

low_findings.title: Static Pattern False Positives in Markdown Examples
low_findings.description: The repeated Ruby backtick detections are Markdown code fence delimiters or inline examples rather than executable Ruby. The weak cryptography, network reconnaissance, and system reconnaissance alerts have no supporting semantic evidence in SKILL.md.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The relevant lines are documentation text, fenced examples, or setup guidance. I did not find executable Ruby, cryptographic code, network probing, or system reconnaissance logic.

dangerous_patterns.title: Command Hook Execution
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill provides Claude hook configuration that executes local Python scripts automatically on tool-use lifecycle events.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The JSON example contains command hook entries and concrete Python script paths. This is a direct automation pattern with security impact.

dangerous_patterns.title: Recursive Deletion Command
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill includes an rm -rf command for deleting hidden Daem0n-MCP data during a reset workflow.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The destructive command is explicit. The example path is scoped, but recursive deletion remains risky operational guidance.

content.value_statement: Teams often lose project decisions when work spans several repositories. This skill explains how to initialize Daem0n-MCP, link related repositories, consolidate memory, and tune search behavior.
actual_capabilities: Explains single-repository and multi-repository Daem0n-MCP setup flows.
Shows how to link related repositories for shared memory recall.
Describes how to consolidate child project databases into a parent project.
Documents condensed recall, enhanced search tuning, and automatic tag inference.
Provides Claude hook examples for memory reminders and automatic decision capture.
Lists health and indexing checks for code entity search features.
limitations: It is documentation only and does not install Daem0n-MCP by itself.
Hook examples depend on local scripts that are not included in this skill.
Repository paths are examples and must be adapted carefully.
Destructive reset commands require manual backup and review before use.

use_cases.title: Unify Memory Across Client and Server Repositories
use_cases.description: Set up a parent Daem0n-MCP project so decisions from related repositories can be recalled together.

use_cases.title: Migrate a Messy Existing Memory Store
use_cases.description: Review, back up, and consolidate existing Daem0n-MCP data before adopting a cleaner parent-project layout.

use_cases.title: Tune Recall for Large Codebases
use_cases.description: Use condensed recall, hybrid search settings, and health checks to keep memory search useful at scale.

prompt_templates.title: Start a Single Repository Setup
prompt_templates.prompt: Guide me through initializing Daem0n-MCP for this single repository. Include only the checks I should run before saving new memories.

prompt_templates.title: Plan a Multi-Repository Memory Layout
prompt_templates.scenario: Client and server repositories share one product context
prompt_templates.prompt: Help me choose between a consolidated parent project and linked separate projects for my client and server repositories.

prompt_templates.title: Review Existing Daem0n-MCP Data
prompt_templates.scenario: Migration from an older or messy setup
prompt_templates.prompt: Walk me through backing up, reviewing, and deciding whether to consolidate or reset my current Daem0n-MCP data.

prompt_templates.title: Design a Safe Hook Automation Plan
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced workflow with Claude hook automation
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a cautious plan for enabling Daem0n-MCP Claude hooks. Include what I should verify before automatic scripts run.

output_examples.input: I have separate backend and frontend repositories under one product folder.
output_examples.output: Use the parent folder as the shared memory root. Link each child repository first, then consolidate only after confirming the memory counts and backup location.

output_examples.input: My memory store has old decisions from unrelated experiments.
output_examples.output: Back up the hidden data directory, review recent memories, keep useful history if it still matches the project, and reset only after approval.

output_examples.input: Search results are too long for a large project.
output_examples.output: Use condensed recall for broad discovery, then switch to full recall when you need rationale, failure history, or implementation details.

best_practices: Back up hidden Daem0n-MCP data before consolidation or reset steps.
Use one parent memory root for repositories that belong to the same product.
Review Claude hook commands and local script paths before enabling automation.
anti_patterns: Do not run recursive deletion commands without a verified backup.
Do not enable automatic hooks from an untrusted local installation.
Do not merge unrelated repositories into one memory store.

faq.question: Does this skill install Daem0n-MCP?
faq.answer: No. It documents setup and migration workflows for an existing Daem0n-MCP installation.

faq.question: Can it support separate client and server repositories?
faq.answer: Yes. It explains both consolidated parent memory and linked separate memory patterns.

faq.question: Why is this skill marked high risk?
faq.answer: It includes Claude hook configuration that automatically runs local Python commands during AI tool events.

faq.question: Is the recursive delete command always unsafe?
faq.answer: It is scoped to an example hidden project directory, but it can destroy data if used without review.

faq.question: Does the skill contain evidence of network exfiltration?
faq.answer: No evidence found in SKILL.md for network exfiltration or credential collection.

faq.question: What should users verify before using hooks?
faq.answer: Users should verify the local Daem0n-MCP installation, script contents, script paths, and backup status before enabling hooks.
